name: Shahab-3 First Test (1998)
description: In July 1998, Iran conducted the first test flight of the Shahab-3 (شهاب ۳), a medium-range ballistic missile developed under [Hassan Tehrani Moghaddam](/people/hassan-tehrani-moghaddam)'s direction. Based on the North Korean Nodong-1 design, the Shahab-3 had an estimated range of 1,300–1,500 km — sufficient to reach Israel, Saudi Arabia, and U.S. military bases in the Gulf region. The test was a major escalation in Iran's deterrence posture and prompted immediate concern from Western governments and Israel. Further tests followed in 2000 and 2003 before the missile entered operational service with the [IRGC Aerospace Force](/orgs/irgc-aerospace-force). The Shahab-3 formed the backbone of Iran's strategic deterrent for over a decade and was the platform from which later variants, including the Ghadr-110 and Emad, were derived.
editorial_notes: First test conducted 22 July 1998. Development relied heavily on North Korean Nodong technology and Russian technical assistance. Moghaddam's direct role is confirmed by multiple post-2011 IRGC tributes and testimonies.
